data.title: Brookdale: Operational Leverage Signals A Major Pivot
data.description:
I believe that Brookdale Senior Living (BKD) has now reached an important turning point in time where the theoretical macro thesis has started to turn into real-world financial performance. The company is no longer operating in stabilization mode, but is starting to operate in a mode of operational leverage, and as such will see all incremental revenue flow directly to the bottom-line. Although the market remains cautious as it is still recovering from many years of extreme volatility within the industry, the data clearly shows that Brookdale is implementing a structural turnaround - one that will be sustainable.

Recent Financials

Brookdale's net loss of $114.7 million for Q3 2025, may be misleading to those who view this as a widening loss compared to last year. A closer review of the results shows that the net loss includes a non-cash impairment charge of $62.7 million related to expected dispositions of property. While this non-cash item will negatively affect Brookdale's results, in terms of identifying active portfolio management and decision-making regarding Brookdale's disposition strategy to preserve future margins, this is a positive signal. Additionally, when adjusting for this non-cash charge and reviewing the adjusted EBITDA, the underlying health of Brookdale's core business can be seen. Adjusted EBITDA has grown by 20.4 percent to $111.1 million over the same period last year. This difference, between Brookdale's net loss and increasing EBITDA, is indicative of the capital intensive nature of Brookdale's turnaround strategy, and the substantial depreciation and other non-recurring charges associated with Brookdale's operations, are masking Brookdale's true earnings power.

The demonstration of operating leverage is the most compelling feature of Brookdale's recent financials. Brookdale's same community operating income has increased by 6.0 percent compared to the prior year period. The same community operating income is an important metric, as it demonstrates the stability and the performance of Brookdale's core asset portfolio excluding transactional activity. Brookdale's same community operating income has been driven primarily through a strong combination of increases in occupancy and rate preservation. Brookdale's consolidated weighted average occupancy had increased to 81.8 percent, which represents a 290 basis point increase year-over-year. In an industry with very high fixed costs, an increase in occupancy of this magnitude will expand Brookdale's margins exponentially, due to the fact that the incremental resident has a much higher revenue-to-cost ratio than the fixed costs of the community.

Finally, the cash flow statement supports the increase in EBITDA. Brookdale's net cash provided by operating activities has improved by $10.1 million to $76.5 million over the same period last year, and Brookdale's adjusted free cash flow has improved by $7.9 million to $21.8 million. Brookdale's ability to produce positive free cash flow is the ultimate validation of Brookdale's turnaround thesis. Positive free cash flow gives Brookdale the flexibility to reinvest in its existing communities rather than needing to rely upon outside capital markets. The fact that Brookdale was able to achieve these improvements while preserving pricing integrity, further supports the notion that the demand Brookdale is experiencing is durable and not the product of discounting to fill beds.

The Debt Refinancing Catalyst

Brookdale has been able to refinance all of its outstanding 2026 mortgage debt obligations (approximately $350 million), while addressing a significant amount (approximately $200 million) of its 2027 maturities. As such, the announcement of the refinancing transactions eliminates the risk of a liquidity crisis or the ability to refinance its wall of maturities from the investment thesis for the immediate future.

In addition to the amount of the new debt, the structure of the new debt is equally important. Brookdale secured a significant amount of the new debt through agency lending programs with Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac. Agency lenders are known to have very strict underwriting standards related to the quality of the assets securing the loan, as well as the creditworthiness of the borrower. The fact that Brookdale was able to secure $245.8 million of the new debt through a Fannie Mae facility, and $146.1 million through a Freddie Mac program represents a significant institutional endorsement of the value of the underlying real estate held in the portfolio.

Additionally, the refinancing of Brookdale's existing debt has resulted in a greater proportion of the company's total debt being comprised of fixed-rate obligations. For example, the Freddie Mac loans have a fixed interest rate of 5.48%, and the majority of the Fannie Mae loans have a fixed interest rate of 5.69%. Therefore, when interest rates remain volatile at the macro level, the company has locked in fixed interest rates for terms of up to 2033 and 2036, providing the company with cost certainty and protecting its future cash flows.

Occupancy Trends and Market Dynamics

The December 2025 Occupancy Data was recently released, and it confirms that the third quarter's momentum is increasing, as opposed to slowing down. Brookdale reported a December Consolidated Weighted Average Occupancy of 82.4 percent, a 310 basis point increase year-over-year. Additionally, there was only a 10 basis point Sequential Weighted Average Occupancy decrease from November to December. Historically, the senior living industry experiences a larger decline in occupancy during the winter months due to the holiday season and seasonal illnesses such as the flu, which typically decrease move-ins and increase attrition. Therefore, outperforming this historical seasonal trend indicates that the underlying demand for the senior living services provided by Brookdale is sufficient to counteract these seasonal headwinds.

The resilience of Brookdale's occupancy is occurring on top of an extremely constrained supply environment. The senior living construction pipeline has collapsed due to high material costs, labor shortages, and tight financing markets for new developments. As a result of this collapse, we have a situation where the 80-plus population cohort is experiencing its largest increase in history, while the growth rate for new inventory is among the lowest in history. This extreme imbalance of supply and demand will create a favorable pricing environment for incumbent operators, such as Brookdale, who have the scale to capture this demand, without having to incur capital expenditure risks associated with developing property from the ground up.

Additionally, Brookdale is experiencing the benefits of its needs-based strategy. Brookdale's portfolio is weighted 23% higher in assisted living and memory care, compared to the industry mix. These two areas of the business are different from independent living, which can be discretionary and correlated to the housing market, because seniors often sell their homes to fund entry into independent living. On the other hand, assisted living and memory care are driven by medical necessity, which reduces Brookdale's correlation to the broader housing economy and creates a stickier resident base. The continued improvement in occupancy, to reach 82.5% for the fourth quarter of 2025, confirms that families are prioritizing the care provided by Brookdale, despite the broader economic uncertainty.

Operational Excellence and Clinical Differentiation

Brookdale's strategic plan goes far beyond merely filling beds; the company is attempting to create a clinical moat in a fragmented marketplace. Brookdale has heavily invested in its Brookdale HealthPlus initiative, designed to provide care coordination services within the community. Brookdale has found success with this strategy; Brookdale HealthPlus residents have experienced 80% fewer urgent care visits, and 66% fewer hospitalizations, compared to other senior living residents residing in their own private homes. Brookdale's clinical value proposition will be critical in protecting their ability to maintain rates.

When families view an aging community as a source of health care solutions instead of simply a place to rent, family members become much less concerned with pricing and are much more interested in health care outcomes. In addition to the clinical value proposition, Brookdale's focus on higher acuity care creates a barrier to entry for other developers who may attempt to replicate Brookdale's clinical infrastructure, regulatory compliance, and specialized staff. As such, the economies of scale that Brookdale enjoys due to its large number of residents allow the company to spread the costs associated with the clinical investments Brookdale makes, thereby creating a competitive cost advantage for Brookdale relative to smaller, regional providers.

Additionally, Brookdale is experiencing improved employee retention, which is essential to providing quality care to residents. A stable workforce promotes positive relationships with residents, which in turn enhances length of stay (a critical measure of profitability). Brookdale is also optimizing its physical footprint. The sale of the 25 communities referred to in the third-quarter impairment charge reflects Brookdale's commitment to prudent capital allocation. By eliminating unproductive locations or markets in which Brookdale cannot achieve density or profitability, Brookdale is able to concentrate its resources on its most productive, high-margin properties, thereby improving the overall margin profile of the consolidated portfolio and minimizing the drag created by underperforming properties.

Valuation Context

Brookdale is trading at a forward EV/Sales of 2.61, representing a 32% discount to the sector median. Perhaps even more compelling is Brookdale's Price/Cash Flow of 13.73, representing a 13% discount to the sector median of 15.83. Given these valuation disconnects, it appears that the marketplace views Brookdale as a distressed asset, rather than as a rapidly expanding entity with strong operational leverage. While the EV/EBITDA of 18.18 is higher than the sector median of 12.91, this premium can be rationalized by Brookdale's high growth rate of EBITDA - up 20% from one year ago, exceeding many other slow-growing REIT peers. As Brookdale continues to utilize its net operating losses to shield cash taxes and take advantage of its fixed cost base, the attractiveness of Brookdale's cash flow yields will likely grow substantially greater than those of the broader sector.

Risks and Counterarguments

I would say that the biggest challenge facing the senior living sector as a whole will be labor. Although labor turnover has been improving, the absolute expense of labor is increasing. Wages increase due to rising demand for healthcare workers from competitors, higher minimum wages, and changes in how overtime is compensated can reduce the operating margin of Brookdale if revenue per available unit does not grow at least as fast as the costs associated with those changes. Brookdale's ability to pass along the cost of labor to its customers is high but not unlimited. If labor increases faster than the increases in RevPAR over an extended period, then my operating leverage thesis would deteriorate.

While there is certainly protection provided by the needs-based nature of assisted living (the senior has no other choice but to enter), there is still a risk that the housing market will freeze or decline significantly. This could cause seniors to delay entering into senior living and thus slow the growth of occupancy. Also, although Brookdale has successfully addressed the 2026 maturities of their debt, Brookdale is still burdened with a very large amount of total debt. Therefore, if interest rates were to continue to climb, it could become increasingly difficult to refinance their debt maturing after 2027, and this could use up what little cash Brookdale generates that could be used to return capital to shareholders or to fund future growth.

Regulatory risk in this space cannot be ignored either. The potential for reimbursement rates to decrease or for strict guidelines to be implemented regarding staffing levels could create additional expenses for the company. Additionally, Brookdale operates in a highly litigious environment. While legal costs are typically removed when calculating Adjusted EBITDA, they represent actual cash outlays that negatively impact the company's bottom line. Finally, the company relies upon third-party referral agencies to obtain referrals for a portion of the move-in process. To maximize the value of each customer acquired by the company, the cost of acquiring that customer must be kept under control.

Future Outlook

The confluence of operational discipline, demographics, and financial engineering is now creating significant value creation opportunities for Brookdale Senior Living. With the clearing of the 2026 debt hurdles, Brookdale has been given one of the most valuable assets of all: time. This will allow the demographic wave of 80-plus seniors to drive occupancy up towards the 90 percent mark, which will create strong profit margins for the company. As Brookdale executes on their strategy, the gap between the current price and intrinsic value should narrow. For those with a medium- or longer-term investment horizon, I believe Brookdale presents a practical way to invest in the aging of America with a stronger balance sheet than previously existed.

data.title: Runway Growth Finance: No Compelling Catalysts Yet
data.description:
Overview

As business development companies continue to face pressure, many names are now trading at a deeply discounted valuation. Runway Growth Finance (RWAY) is no exception, as the stock now trades near one of the deepest price-to-NAV levels since its inception.

When I previously covered RWAY several quarters ago, I issued a hold rating due to the improved portfolio fundamentals. Now that we've crossed into the new year, I wanted to reassess RWAY's value proposition since interest rates have declined since my last coverage. Unfortunately, RWAY still faces issues that make the growth potential look fairly limited.

Looking at the performance over the last 12 months, we can see that RWAY's share price has declined by more than 18.5%. RWAY has struggled to regain any positive price momentum as uncertainty around the health of the debt markets increases. Even when including all distributions that were paid out to shareholders, the total return still sits at a loss of around 6.5% over the same time frame.

RWAY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of 15.1%, but I remain cautious about the overall sustainability of the payouts going forward. Net investment coverage has become thin and any additional declines in earnings can cause the distribution to be reduced.

Although the portfolio's level of non-accruals seems healthy, the potential growth catalysts seem limited. RWAY has failed to provide any meaningful improvements to its NAV momentum over the last year, and management has struggled to allocate capital towards new investment opportunities. As long as interest rates remain high, I believe that RWAY will continue to slowly erode investor capital over time. So let's start by first taking a look at the portfolio strategy and most recent financials to get an understanding of the BDC's health.

Financials

According to the latest portfolio breakdown, RWAY has total investments at a fair value of $945.9M. This is a slight decline from 2024's year end total fair value of $1.07B. Unlike traditional equities, RWAY doesn't rely on the earnings growth of its positions to see positive results. Instead, RWAY essentially lends capital out to middle market companies and collects interest or equity in exchange. Therefore, RWAY is essentially making a bet that its portfolio companies will be able to keep up with their required debt maintenance. The majority of RWAY's portfolio is structured as senior secured first lien loans, but the entire portfolio is broken down as follows:

  • Senior Secured First Lien Term Loan: 91%
  • Preferred Stock: 4%
  • Second Lien Term Loan: 2%
  • Warrant: 2%
  • Equity Interest: 1%

Expanding on this, RWAY also maintains an efficient level of diversity across different industries. This helps reduce concentration risk to any one specific industry. RWAY is most heavily allocated to Application Software investments at 24% of total assets. This is followed by an allocation to Systems Software and Health Care Equipment & Services, which accounts for 15% and 13% of assets, respectively. However, it is clear that RWAY is more heavily skewed towards technology-focused investments, which may create volatility if the technology sector experiences a pullback.

RWAY reported its Q3 earnings at the start of November, and the results were a bit mixed. Most notably, net investment income totaled $0.43 per share, which is equivalent to $15.7M. Ultimately, there is very little shift from the prior year's numbers. For instance, total investment income landed at $36.7M, which was a very marginal increase over the prior year's total investment income of $36.6M. Similarly, total operating expenses landed at $21M, which was also a very marginal increase from the prior year's total operating expenses of $20.7M.

The biggest change was the fact that RWAY experienced net realized losses of $7.7M for the quarter, which was a large decline from the prior year's net realized gains of $9.1M. This can be an indication that the portfolio companies are struggling to grow their earnings in this high-interest-rate environment. Another notable aspect is the portfolio's decline of its average yield, which now sits at 13%. This is a decline from the prior year's yield of 14.4%, which indicates that the portfolio is simply generating less income than it once was.

On a more positive note, RWAY was able to see some structural improvements on its balance sheet. For instance, cash and equivalents now sit at $7.9M, which was an improvement from the prior year-end total of $5.7M. Similarly, total debt has declined to $443.5M, which was a decline from $552.3M. This ultimately puts RWAY in a healthier position to continue navigating these challenging market conditions.

Valuation & Risks

At the time of my last coverage, RWAY traded at a large discount to NAV of 33%. Despite the share price eroding over recent months, RWAY now trades at a slightly smaller discount to NAV of 29.88%. For reference, RWAY traded at an average discount to NAV of 15.3% over the last five years. Referring to the red line on the graph below, we can see that RWAY continues to trade at one of the deepest discount to NAV valuations over its historical range. However, I believe this large discount is a direct reflection of the challenges that RWAY continues to face. Therefore, this should not be mistaken as an attractive opportunity to accumulate shares.

One of the main challenges has been RWAY's inability to allocate a meaningful amount of capital towards new investment opportunities. For instance, RWAY was able to allocate $128.3M, which was allocated to a total of 11 investments, including new and existing portfolio companies. However, this was offset by sales and repayments totaling $199.7M for the quarter, which ultimately resulted in a net decline for the quarter. The most notable aspect of this failure to grow its base portfolio is that the underlying NAV continues to decline. For reference, the quarter-end NAV amounts are as follows:

  • Q4 2024: $13.79 per share
  • Q1 2025: $13.48 per share
  • Q2 2025: $13.66 per share
  • Q3 2025: $13.55 per share

So despite RWAY's non-accruals remaining healthy, the main risk comes from the continuous NAV decline if the portfolio companies cannot grow. According to the last earnings call, management confirms that there is only one loan on non-accrual status. Non-accruals represent the amount of portfolio companies that cannot keep up with their required debt maintenance and aren't actively contributing towards a BDC's earnings. The active non-accrual has a cost basis of $4.8M and represents 0.2% of fair portfolio value. This is extremely low when compared to some of the most notable BDCs in the sector. For reference, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs:

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Blue Owl Capital (OBDC): 1.3% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.
  • Hercules Capital (HTGC): 1.1% non-accrual rate at fair portfolio value.

The biggest challenge will remain the higher interest rate environment. Despite the Fed initiating three separate 25 basis point cuts to interest rates throughout 2025, the federal funds rate remains near the higher end of its ten-year range. A higher interest rate will ultimately limit the number of businesses seeking debt financing, since the cost of that debt is elevated. This can limit the new investment opportunities for RWAY and continue to put pressure on existing portfolio companies that have thin operating margins.

Dividend Coverage

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.33 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 15.1%. As we've established, net investment income amounted to $0.43 per share for the quarter. This indicates that the earnings support the distribution at a coverage rate of 130%. Therefore, the distributions are likely to remain consistent throughout 2026. Furthermore, management confirmed that they closed the last quarter with $0.53 in undistributed spillover income. Despite this, I would still suggest caution for investors seeking a reliable source of income generation within their portfolio.

After covering tons of BDCs over the last few quarters, I've witnessed how rapidly the portfolio health can change. Any further deterioration in RWAY's portfolio companies can drastically impact earnings and cause net investment income to decline. If the portfolio continues to see sales and repayments that outpace new investment activity, this will cause earnings to decline as well.

Lastly, investors should take into consideration that the distributions received from RWAY do not offer any tax efficiency. Since distributions are being supported by net investment income, the payouts are likely classified as ordinary dividends, which have the greatest tax burden for investors. Therefore, a position in RWAY would be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account for investors that want to minimize their overall tax liability.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that RWAY remains a hold at this time. I see no compelling catalysts at this time to help propel the share price or earnings higher. The management struggles to allocate a meaningful level of capital towards new investment activity, which means that the NAV will continue to erode over time. Overall, I see no compelling reason to buy shares at this time, but I also don't think there are any clear red flags that warrant existing shareholders selling out of their position. RWAY will likely be able to continue navigating this current high-interest-rate environment, since distributions are still supported by earnings.

data.title: U.S. IPO Weekly Recap: Trio Of Sizable Listings Underwhelm To Close Out January
data.description:
Four IPOs and ten SPACs priced this week. Three IPOs and five SPACs submitted filings.

Satellite manufacturer York Space Systems (YSS) priced its upsized IPO at the top of the range to raise $629 million at a $4.3 billion market cap. York is a space and defense primary contractor, providing design, manufacturing, integration, and operation of spacecraft platforms. York is the number one provider to the Department of Defense PWSA program by spacecraft operating in orbit (33), number of contracts (6), and variety of contract types. The company is the first and only name to demonstrate Link-16 connectivity from space and has an order backlog of over $640 million, although it is unprofitable and faces significant competition from new and legacy defense contractors. York Space Systems finished the week flat.

Brazilian digital bank PicS (PICS) priced its US IPO at the top of the range to raise $434 million at a $2.5 billion market cap. PicS operating under the name PicPay, provides a digital financial services platform in Brazil for consumers (76% of 9mo25 net revenue) and SMEs (18%), catering to both consumers and businesses, with 42 million quarterly active consumers and about 812,000 active businesses accepting its payments network as of September 30, 2025. The company has posted strong recent top-line growth, although it faces numerous regulatory risks. PicS finished the week down 5%.

Life insurance platform Ethos Technologies (LIFE) priced its IPO at the midpoint to raise $200 million at a $1.3 billion market cap. The company operates a digital platform that integrates distribution, underwriting, policy issuance, payments, and administration for life insurance. The platform processes applications and servicing for consumers, provides agents with sales and policy management tools, and supports carriers with underwriting and administration. Ethos has posted strong revenue growth and increasing profitability, although just three carriers account for 98% of its revenue. It finished the week down 21%.

Government relations services provider Public Policy Holding Company (PPHC) priced its US IPO at $12.25 to raise $51 million at a $363 million market cap. PPHC is a global communications and public affairs advisory group, providing government relations, corporate communications, and compliance services. The company serves approximately 1,400 active clients worldwide. The company boasts a blue-chip client base with a strong retention rate, although its organic growth has slowed to the mid-single digits in recent periods. PPHC finished the week flat.

Ten SPACs also priced. The largest, Mountain Lake Acquisition II (MLAAU), raised $313 million. Other notable SPAC pricings included Kraken-backed KRAKacquisition (KRAQU), which raised $300 million; M Evo Global II (MEVOU), which raised $270 million to target critical miners; and Avi Katz-backed GigCapital9 (GIXXU), which raised $220 million.

14 IPOs During the Week of January 26th, 2026

Blank check company formed by Globis Capital Advisors founder Paul Packer.

Three IPOs submitted initial filings this week. Option Therapeutics (OPTN), a liver disease biotech spun out of BioVie, filed to raise $25 million. V Capital Consulting (VCCG), a business consulting firm spun out of Malaysia’s VCI Global, filed to raise $15 million. AIAI Holdings (AIAI), a holdings company with a licensed proprietary AI model, filed for a direct listing on the Nasdaq.

Five SPACs also submitted initial filings. Averin Capital Acquisition (ACAAU) filed to raise $250 million to target health and life sciences. Iron Dome Acquisition I (IDACU) filed to raise $200 million to target Israeli tech. ClearThink 1 Acquisition (CTAAU) filed to raise $150 million to target financial services. Paloma Acquisition I (PALOU) filed to raise $150 million to target precious metals. Sky Acquisition Group (SKAIU) filed to raise $150 million to target AI.

8 Filings During the Week of January 26th, 2026

Option Therapeutics (OPTN)

 $25M Health Care ThinkEquity
Phase 2 liver disease biotech spun out of BioVie.

Week Ahead

Seven IPOs raising at least $100 million are currently scheduled for the week ahead, in what could be one of the busiest weeks for sizable offerings since 2021. Notably, only one other week over the past four years has produced seven $100 million-plus IPOs, underscoring the renewed depth in issuance even as IPO investors remain selective. An eighth IPO (SGP) is tentatively scheduled for the month and could also list this week.

The week ahead features emerging themes including a biotech rebound, a consumer comeback, and the first billion-dollar IPO of the year. However, stay tuned on whether a possible US government shutdown impedes their listing plans.

Veradermics Therapeutics (MANE) plans to raise $200 million at a $509 million market cap. The dermatologist-founded, late clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company is developing an oral, non-hormonal extended-release formulation of minoxidil for male and female pattern hair loss. Veradermics has fully enrolled its Phase 2/3 trial in men and has initiated two additional Phase 3 registration-directed trials in male and female patients to support an NDA, targeting a large, underserved population.

Bob's Discount Furniture (BOBS) plans to raise $370 million at a $2.4 billion market cap. The national omnichannel retailer operates more than 200 showrooms and offers value-focused home furnishings through a tiered pricing strategy. Bob’s serves a broad customer base with a high level of cross-channel engagement and rapid delivery capabilities, bolstered by strong regional revenue concentration and an established multi-state footprint.

Eikon Therapeutics (EIKN) plans to raise $318 million at a $908 million valuation. Led by a team of executives from Merck, the late-stage clinical biopharmaceutical company has raised more than $1 billion privately, and is advancing a pipeline of oncology drug candidates, including a TLR7/8 dual agonist in global Phase 2/3 trials and multiple early-stage programs targeting key cancer indications. Eikon’s diversified clinical portfolio reflects an ambitious push across immuno-oncology and targeted therapy development.

Forgent Power Solutions (FPS) plans to raise $1.6 billion at an $8.2 billion market cap. The designer and manufacturer of electrical distribution equipment serves data centers, power grids, and industrial facilities with a broad suite of products, including transformers and switchgear. Forgent benefits from a large manufacturing footprint and recent capacity expansion to support growth across energy-intensive end markets, including fast-growing AI data center customers.

Liftoff Mobile (LFTO) plans to raise $762 million at a $5.5 billion market cap. Backed by Blackstone and formed through the merger of Liftoff and Vungle, the company’s AI-powered platform supports mobile app user acquisition and ad monetization across verticals such as gaming, finance, and social media. Its software development kit is integrated into hundreds of thousands of apps, reaching over a billion daily active users.

Once Upon a Farm (OFRM) plans to raise $209 million at a $724 million market cap. Co-founded by actress Jennifer Garner, the producer of organic, refrigerated food products for babies and children sells its nutritious products through retail and online channels, achieving broad distribution and a significant direct-to-consumer presence. Its portfolio includes cold-pressed pouches and snacks designed for convenience and healthy eating.

AgomAb Therapeutics (AGMB) plans to raise $213 million at an $875 million market cap. The clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company is developing novel disease-modifying therapies for immunology and inflammatory diseases, with a lead oral inhibitor targeting the TGF-β pathway in fibrotic conditions. Its pipeline also includes an inhaled therapy for idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis.

The Renaissance IPO Indices are market cap-weighted baskets of newly public companies. As of 1/29/2026, the Renaissance IPO Index was up 1.2% year-to-date, while the S&P 500 was up 1.9%. Renaissance Capital’s IPO ETF (IPO) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include CoreWeave (CRWV) and Kenvue (KVUE). The Renaissance International IPO Index was up 13.7% year-to-date, while the ACWX was up 6.7%. Renaissance Capital’s International IPO ETF (IPOS) tracks the index, and top ETF holdings include Kioxia and Galderma.

data.title: Dutch Parties Reach Minority Coalition Deal
data.description:
By Bert Colijn, Chief Economist, Netherlands and Marieke Blom, Chief Economist and Global Head of Research

Quick formation with challenges to come for new minority government

A new Dutch government is drawing closer as the social liberal D66, Christian Democrats (CDA) and liberal conservative VVD parties have reached a coalition agreement. This means that a formation period will now follow in which a cabinet will be assembled.

The parties expect to be able to have the new government in place by the end of next month. This would mark a significantly faster government formation period than in previous Dutch elections.

The government will face considerable challenges with their agenda from the onset, as they only hold 66 out of 150 seats in parliament. This type of minority government is new for Dutch politics and means that majorities will need to be found across parliament for every proposed policy.

A competitiveness agenda aimed at improving growth prospects

The economic agenda is pro-business, despite increased taxes to pay for higher defence spending. The coalition focuses on lowering energy costs for Dutch manufacturers and subsidies for greening energy production.

The construction sector will benefit from additional funds for housing, while high nitrogen emissions will be reduced largely through financial support for farmers who choose to terminate their business.

It also aims to reduce regulatory burdens for businesses and maintain attractive conditions for expats, to name a few measures. The programme is also more supportive of longer-term innovation and reverses previous cuts to the science and education budget. It actively targets a structural GDP growth rate of 1.5%.

Higher spending on defence comes with cuts elsewhere

Although the numbers have not been run by the Central Planning Bureau yet, the Netherlands looks set to keep its budget deficit within 3% of GDP in the coming government period. This is despite large extra defence spending efforts.

Large cuts to healthcare spending (largely via increased co-payments), social security and higher taxes for households and businesses are a result. With government debt levels already well within the 60% debt-to-GDP EU target, the Dutch are set to remain among the best in class from a European budgetary perspective. Although we do note that longer-term spending plans will also raise budgetary pressure beyond the upcoming period.

A more cooperative stance on Europe

Finally, the incoming government is likely to turn more positive towards the EU. And while it denounces Eurobonds, it only does so using a very narrow definition: it excludes the pooling of national public debt.

However, it remains constructive towards existing instruments for common financing, an important nuance compared to the previous government. It is supportive of the capital market, and banking unions.

A political experiment with ambition

All in all, the Netherlands is set to have a new government rather quickly. The coalition is keen on implementing measures to improve business competitiveness, increase defence spending, according to the new NATO target, and produce significant cuts to healthcare and social security to keep the budgetary impact of increased spending plans limited. Whether they can achieve this will depend on the willingness of the opposition to offer its support. A new political experiment begins.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: LG Display: Going Lower In The Short Term For Several Reasons
data.description:
LG Display (LPL), a manufacturer of display panels, fell substantially after the release of the Q4 2025 report before the market opened on January 28. Still, the report itself was not so bad, but more like a mixed bag because while it was worse than expected in some ways, it also showed some improvements. This makes it rather difficult to gauge the road ahead for LPL, but there are nonetheless several reasons why down could be the more likely one, at least in the short term.

LPL breaks down

A previous article from April 2025 written by myself took note of how the Q1 2025 report, which was released shortly before, showed a lot of improvement. Yet there was reason to be wary because of the prevailing tariff situation at the time, which had the potential to cause problems for a company like LPL with its exposure to the sale of many commonly used consumer devices, especially in light of how the stock had collapsed in the prior weeks. In the end, I chose to rate LPL a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how the stock was able to rebound off of the April 2025 low of $2.43 per ADS to put together a major rally. LPL responded well to the easing of the global tariff dispute, which helped the stock reach a 52-week high of $5.67 in October 2025. However, LPL has declined since then, with most of the losses coming after the release of the last two earnings reports.

Why the latest move is significant

The most recent, or Q4 2025, report was released on January 28, and it was followed by an 8.9% drop in the stock to close at $3.97 per ADS. This marks the first time the stock has made it below the $4.00 price point after staying above it for the last few months.

The stock declined after the October high, as shown in the chart above, but even though there were numerous opportunities, the stock did not trade below $4.00 for months because whenever the stock went down and got close, the stock proceeded to turn north. The closest the stock got was on December 22, when it hit an intraday low of $4.04, before closing the day at $4.09. That is until January 28.

Furthermore, there is reason to believe the move has consequences. It’s worth mentioning that LPL rallied from the April 2025 low of $2.43 to the October 2025 high of $5.67, and the 50% Fibonacci retracement of $2.43 to $5.67 is $4.05. This could explain why the stock was only able to close as low as $4.09, or as low as $4.04 if intraday lows are included, before being forced to turn higher in recent months. LPL is now trading below this level, and assuming it stays there, this suggests support that had held the stock aloft for months has been broken.

This loss of support could herald more lows in the short term. In addition, it is worth mentioning that the recently concluded Q4 2025, or the December quarter, tends to benefit from seasonality due to the holiday shopping season, which tends to boost sales of consumer items utilizing display panels, including those from LPL.

The last quarter of the year tends to be the best by far of the year for this reason. However, seasonality reverses in the first quarter of the new year, or the March quarter, which usually results in quarterly numbers that are much worse than the December quarter. Combine worse results in upcoming reports with a stock without support, and that could power the stock lower in the short term.

Why LPL fell after the latest report

The fact that the stock fell after the Q4 2025 report was not surprising because it included a number of concerning developments. First, the last quarter tends to be the best due to seasonality, as mentioned earlier. Yet there was much less of a seasonal boost than LPL should have seen based on past precedent.

LPL reported a net loss of KRW351B on revenue of KRW7,201B in Q4 2025, which converts to $0.25B and $5.03B using a USD:KRW exchange rate of 1:1,431. This is worse than in the preceding Q3 2025, even with an increase in the top line. To be fair, the bottom line was affected by one-time expenses, but that only partially makes up for the letdown.

Gross margin declined QoQ, but it improved YoY. Operating income doubled YoY, but the QoQ and YoY decrease in EBITDA is disappointing. The decrease in net losses despite less revenue is good to see. Overall the report had some positives, but it was still a mixed bag overall in what is supposed to be the best report of the fiscal year.

The table below shows the numbers for all of 2025. LPL ended 2025 with a net profit of KRW304B or $0.21B on revenue of KRW25,810B or $18.03B, which is better than the net losses in prior years. In general, profitability got significantly better, despite a decrease in revenue. Keep also in mind LPL disposed of its LCD fab in China during the year, which affected shipments and revenue by extension. The sale of the LCD fab to TCL’s CSOT unit was valued at $1.5B.

The table below provides some insights into the latest results. LPL shipped far fewer panels, which makes sense with the sale of the LCD fab, but average selling prices rose because with fewer LCD panels, the proportion of OLED panels increased, and these come with higher prices. Yet the decline in prices in Q4 2025 stands out, which seems to be related to a less favorable product mix.
