The sale of the LCD fab was felt in another way. The balance sheet at LPL has long been in need of improvement, and that’s what happened in 2025 thanks in part to the cash infusions from asset sales. Total assets decreased from KRW32,860B at the end of 2024 to KRW26,917B or $18.8B at the end of 2025, but this was offset by a decrease in liabilities from KRW24,787B in 2024 to KRW19,077B or $13.2B in 2025.

Debt has and continues to be a problem for LPL, but total debt decreased from KRW14,550B in 2024 to KRW12,664B or $8.85B in 2025. Net debt decreased from KRW12,528B in 2024 to KRW11,091B or $7.75B in 2025. Cash and cash equivalents decreased from KRW2,022B in 2024 to KRW1,573B or $1.1B in 2025.

However, one reason why the stock sold off was likely because the balance sheet could get hit by projected spending on capex in 2026. From the Q4 2025 earnings call:

“We maintained a CapEx policy focused on investments in our future readiness and structural upgrade. After investment optimization activities, CapEx in 2025 was completed at mid KRW 1 trillion. In 2026, CapEx is expected at KRW 2 trillion level, up Y-o-Y. This includes execution of the planned investment to enhance OLED technological competitiveness and investment to strengthen OLED business and future readiness.”

At the earnings call, LPL stated its capex budget would increase in 2026 versus 2025, possibly reaching the two trillion KRW range or $1.4+B.

Why 2026 could be a more difficult year for LPL

The year 2025 is over, and there was some progress made, especially in terms of overall profitability. However, there is reason to think 2026 might be more difficult for LPL for several reasons. For starters, the fact that Q4 2025, or the end of 2025, got significantly less of a seasonal boost than it should have gotten based on historical patterns does not bode well for 2026.

LPL should benefit from there being a couple of major sporting events in 2026, like the 2026 Winter Olympics and especially the 2026 FIFA World Cup. Major sporting events tend to boost demand for big-screen TVs, which works in favor of LPL as the primary supplier of OLED display panels for OLED TVs, which are preferred by those that value picture quality.

However, the Q4 2025 results could be hinting that there will not be much of a boost, if at all, in 2026. Furthermore, this tailwind, if it happens, could get swamped by headwinds elsewhere. This is because memory prices have rocketed higher, and this could negatively affect sales of devices with display panels, many of which use memory chips. This includes smartphones, tablets, PCs, TVs, and many other devices.

DRAM prices, for instance, are roughly four times what they were a year ago, and this will affect prices of products that use a lot of memory. It may take a while for the effect to filter through, but this is likely to impact sales of other components used in these devices, and that includes display panels provided by LPL. The Q4 2025 results could be a hint that this process may already be underway.

Why CES 2026 showed LPL could have a tough time competing in the future

LPL’s sale of the LCD fab is reflective of the importance attached to the OLED business at the expense of the LCD business. According to LPL, OLED revenue now accounts for 61% of total revenue, or almost twice the 32% share it had in 2020. However, it is possible LPL bet on the wrong horse by going with OLED over LCD.

For instance, at the latest CES held earlier in January, OEMs, including LPL, had an opportunity to display the latest and greatest, and what was shown does not bode well for LPL in my opinion. The definition of “best” is subjective, but based on personal observations from yours truly, LCD technology may actually have the advantage over OLED at this time.

The overall impression is that while OLED is still tops in terms of overall image quality, like contrast due to having true black, the gap with LCD has shrunk a lot. Compare to say five years ago, LCD has made much bigger strides in terms of improving picture quality than OLED. There is still a difference, but it is such a small one that most people won’t think about it, unless they watch an LCD TV next to an OLED TV, something people usually don’t do.

The latest high-end miniLED TVs with the latest features like quantum dot and thousands of dimming zones can come very close to matching the best of OLED. Meanwhile, miniLED TVs targeting the same consumer segment as their equivalent OLED counterparts are much cheaper. For instance, while it depends on various factors like brand and specific features, most of the better, for instance, 65-inch units are in the $1,000+ range, or roughly half the $2,000+ for OLED counterparts.

One could see how potential buyers shopping for a new TV could opt to forego the small difference in image quality provided by OLED to save money. The savings could also be used to purchase a much bigger LCD TV with all the bells and whistles. Instead of a 65-inch unit, someone could get an 85-inch or even bigger unit for the same money it would have taken to go with the OLED option.

Sure, you have to give up a small amount of image quality, but in return you get much more screen size since, for instance, an 85-inch TV has 71% more display area than a 65-inch unit. That difference is something people will definitely notice all the time, something not necessarily true of an OLED TV versus a miniLED TV when it comes to picture quality.

This is just one example of why the possibility OLED could eventually lose out to LCD in the long run should not be ruled out. Both LCD and OLED are evolving, and it remains to be seen what the future holds. At this time, it seems like LCD is doing a better job making inroads into those areas where OLED is strong than vice versa. This is not a positive trend for a company like LPL.

Investor takeaways

The year 2025 was better for LPL than prior years. LPL even managed to end 2025 with a small net profit, a rarity for LPL in recent years, but the way LPL ended the year with a soft Q4 2025 is cause for concern. The report seems to have caused the stock to drop below support that had prevented the stock from moving lower, which could pave the way for a further decline in the stock.

Furthermore, there are several other factors that could contribute to pushing the stock lower. Quarterly results will get worse after the latest report due to seasonality. LPL intends to raise capex spending this year at a time the balance sheet is not in great shape. It’s possible LPL may have to borrow or raise capital, especially if the income statement gets worse.

That the income statement could get worse is quite possible due to the spike in memory prices, especially DRAM. This could raise prices of many consumer goods in 2026, which could negatively affect sales, including those of components that go into them, like display panels. LPL does have the benefit of there being a couple of major sporting events in 2026, which could boost demand to the point it offsets weakness elsewhere, but there is no guarantee this is going to happen.

Longer term, there is also reason to question whether LPL’s bet on OLED will turn out to be a mistake. The recent CES 2026 suggests that while OLED and LCD tech are making progress, the latter seems to be doing so faster. For most people, a TV using miniLED and not OLED is the better option overall, in my opinion. If LCD eventually triumphs over OLED, LPL could pay a heavy price for backing the wrong horse.

I am neutral on LPL with all the above in mind. There are several reasons why LPL could be forced to trade lower, especially in the short term. Whether it is the charts, the likelihood the income statement and balance sheet are more likely to get worse than better short term, or the fact that OLED seems to be much closer to its technological peak than LCD. There is much to ponder about before thinking of going long LPL.

data.title: Will The RBA Opt For A Rapid Policy Reversal?
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

The latest inflation data from Australia has once again raised concerns among monetary policymakers and investors. Core inflation (the trimmed mean) - regarded by the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) as the key measure of underlying price pressure - rose in the fourth quarter to 3.4% year-on-year. This reading not only exceeded market expectations (3.3%), but also moved above the upper bound of the RBA’s inflation target range (2–3%).

On a quarterly basis, core inflation came in at 0.9%, in line with forecasts. However, the annual acceleration suggests that the disinflation process may be less durable than previously assumed.

Headline inflation accelerates – energy and services in focus

Price pressures are also evident in headline inflation. In December, CPI rose to 3.8% y/y, up from 3.4% a month earlier. The largest contributors to the increase were:

  • Housing: +5.5%
  • Food and non-alcoholic beverages: +3.4%
  • Recreation and culture: +4.4%

The composition of inflation deserves particular attention. Services inflation accelerated to 4.1% y/y, from 3.6%, typically signalling strong domestic demand and persistent wage pressures. Goods inflation, meanwhile, stood at 3.4% y/y, with electricity prices surging by 21.5%, further complicating efforts to rein in inflation.

Labour market strength strengthens the case for tighter policy

Elevated inflation is accompanied by a relatively tight labour market. Unemployment has fallen to around 4%, suggesting that demand-side pressures in the economy remain strong.

This macroeconomic mix — high inflation and low unemployment — significantly narrows the central bank’s room for manoeuvre and increases the risk that price pressures become entrenched.

It is worth recalling that the RBA cut interest rates as recently as August, but already signalled in December that the next move could be a rate hike if inflation data proved concerning.

Financial markets price in a February rate hike

Market reaction has been swift. OIS contracts now price in around a 76% probability of a rate hike at the 2–3 February meeting. Such a move would represent a sharp pivot in monetary policy - less than six months after the last rate cut.

At the same time, three-year government bond yields fell to 4.28%, possibly indicating that some investors view the recent rise in inflation as temporary or expect only a one-off tightening move by the RBA.

Among major financial institutions, consensus is building around the upcoming meeting. Westpac Banking Corp. (WEBNF) and ANZ Bank (ANZGY) both expect a 25-basis-point rate hike, taking the cash rate to 3.85%.

Westpac, however, stresses that such a move does not necessarily mark the start of a sustained tightening cycle. A “wait-and-see” approach remains plausible, particularly if inflationary pressures prove short-lived and begin to ease in the months ahead.

A stronger Australian dollar signals investor confidence

Within the G10 currency universe, the Australian dollar has performed notably well. Since the start of the year, it has appreciated by over 4%, making it the second-best-performing currency in the group. The currency’s strength reflects both expectations of higher interest rates and investor confidence in Australia’s relative resilience amid global economic uncertainty.

The RBA decision as a test of credibility

The upcoming RBA meeting in early February will carry significance beyond a single interest-rate decision. Following a recent easing cycle, the central bank now faces a credibility test: whether to pivot decisively in response to rising inflation or treat the latest data as a temporary disturbance.

What is clear is that the recent inflation readings have substantially increased pressure on the RBA, and the February decision could set the tone for Australian monetary policy in the quarters ahead.

Original Post

data.title: Apollo Commercial: Interesting Transaction In The CRE Space
data.description:
Thesis

Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance, Inc. (ARI) is a CRE REIT we have not covered before, although we have written articles on most of its peers from the space. A recent market development that is very interesting in today's context is prompting us to look at ARI.

Who is ARI?

As per the Seeking Alpha landing page, ARI:

Operates as a real estate investment trust that originates, acquires, invests in, and manages commercial first mortgage loans, subordinate financings, and other commercial real estate-related debt investments. It is qualified as a REIT under the Internal Revenue Code.

ARI falls in the same category as a couple of other names we have covered in the past:

  • KKR Real Estate Finance Trust: Trying To Lend Their Way Out Of The Rut
  • Ares Commercial: No Light At The End Of This Tunnel
  • Blackstone Mortgage: Buy After Solid Earnings, Distributions Now Supported

ARI falls in the CRE Reit category, with a very sizable discount to NAV:

For the past two years, the market has not believed the book value posted by ARI, thus trading the name with a 20% to 40% discount to book.

What is happening with ARI?

ARI announced on January 28, 2026 that it was selling its commercial real estate loan portfolio to Athene Holding:

NEW YORK, Jan. 28, 2026 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) -- Apollo Commercial Real Estate Finance, Inc. today announced the Company has entered into a definitive agreement with Athene Holding Ltd. (“Athene”) to sell the Company’s entire approximately $9 billion commercial real estate loan portfolio for a purchase price based on 99.7% of total loan commitments, net of asset specific CECL reserves, and with the exception of two loans with a combined total principal balance of $146 million that are expected to be repaid prior to closing.

This is virtually the entire fund portfolio, but noting that the real estate owned assets ('REO') are not part of the sale. If we look at the last reported balance sheet we can see the sale basically cleans that up:

The company is motivating this decision by the persistent discount to book value for the common shares throughout time:

One of the stated goals of the entity going forward is to position the REIT so that they trade at or above book value. Cleaning up the entire balance sheet that was issued in the past is most certainly a way to do it.

To note however that Athene also falls under the larger Apollo umbrella. Apollo acquired Athene in 2022 for $11 billion. We are not commenting anything on the exit price, but one must wonder if a truly non-related party would not have bargained for a lower price point.

Assets are going, liabilities are going too

The agreed transaction really cleans up the entire balance sheet for the company, virtually giving it a new start:

Upon the close of the transaction and the paydown of its repurchase lines, senior secured notes, and term loan B, ARI's capital structure will consist of approximately $1.4 billion in cash, real estate owned (REO) assets that were valued at $466 million as of Sept. 30, 2025 (net of debt), $169 million in preferred stock, and $1.7 billion in equity.

Virtually all of the company debt supporting the investment portfolio will be repaid as well, leaving the REIT with a small pool of REO buildings, its preferred stock and cash. The Series B preferred equity are privately placed securities, thus not traded on an exchange.

The transaction has been approved by the ARI board and is expected to close in the second half of 2026:

We expect the transaction to close in the second quarter of 2026, subject to shareholder approval, customary regulatory approvals, and the satisfaction of customary closing conditions. During this transition period, ARI will pay Apollo Global a reduced annual management fee in common stock equal to 75 basis points on net equity.

We expect common shareholders to vote in favor of the transaction since it creates a massive benefit via the closing out of the discount to book value. The company estimates book value to be $12.05 per share post-transaction:

After repayment of substantially all financing facilities, other indebtedness and estimated transaction expenses, we expect ARI to have a common equity book value per share of approximately $12.05.

Since the common shares are still trading with a discount, do expect the equity to move up closer to book value, but with a modest -5% discount to account for uncertainties.

The Company future is uncertain, but the dividend remains for now

The company has stated that it is deciding what to do next, and will announce something by the end of 2026. Until then the dividend remains:

We fully intend for ARI to continue to qualify as a REIT for tax purposes. And as such, we expect ARI will pay a first quarter dividend of $0.25 per share, consistent with the recent quarterly dividend level subject to Board approval. In addition, ARI intends to continue paying a quarterly dividend subject to Board approval targeting an approximately 8% annualized yield based on post transaction book value per share.

The company will keep paying the current $0.25 dividend per share until a new strategy is devised:

It is interesting to note they even mentioned dissolution in their presentation. We do not think it will come to that. Apollo is a company that works on fees, and now that ARI has cleaned-up their balance sheet, we are certain they will find a niche in the CRE sector. We also see that the preferred equity is not being redeemed, further telling us the name is keeping cheap sources of funding because it wants to continue as an ongoing entity.

Our take on the transaction and the forward

To us this looks like a means to 'start over' for ARI. They exited basically their entire book close to par and only have a few REOs to button up. The transaction provides a clean slate for new vintage mortgages and thus does not give the market a reason to trade the name at a discount. We do not think the REIT will dissolve, and they will eventually come up with a new bellicose strategy involving CRE loans. They are keeping their cheap preferred shares alive just for that, and will continue to clip fees in the future.

For common shareholders, we are expecting a pull closer to that $12/share book value, with a small discount for all existing uncertainties (potential losses on remaining REO, transaction not closing, etc.). For us, a -5% discount would be fair.

Conclusion

ARI is a CRE REIT undergoing a strategic transformation. The entity has agreed to sell its entire mortgage loan book to Athene, for a price close to par. Going forward, the debt will be paid down as well if the transaction closes successfully, meaning the REIT will run a massive cash balance. The entity is keeping just its REO assets, which are roughly netted at $0.5 billion. The REIT is not cutting its dividend and is undergoing a strategic review for 2026 to decide its future. We do not think they will close down, and we see the common shares moving closer to the projected book value, albeit with a small discount to account for uncertainties.

data.title: Butler National: The Strangest Aerospace Stock And Why It's Still A Buy
data.description:
I have been covering Butler National Corporation (BUKS) for a while now. The company has exposure to the aerospace and defense industries but in a niche while it also has a rather odd gambling business. The stock, however, has performed well since my last report reaching my price target. This brings the total return to 88.7% since I upgraded the stock from hold to buy. In this report, I am revisiting the investment case for Butler.

Since Butler National Corporation has a rather interesting combination of businesses, I have appended the company description from my previous report and updated with the most recent figures where applicable. If you are not familiar with the company, I highly recommend reading the description below:

Butler National Corporation Has Exposure To Aerospace And Gambling

Butler National Corporation was established in 1960. The company is headquartered in New Century, Kansas, and has a total of 360 employees, so it is not a huge company. Its market cap is also only $161.7 million. That, however, does not mean that I am not interested in covering the company or considering it for investment.

The company reports through two segments, namely Aerospace Products and Professional Services. The aerospace and defense products segment operates through four subsidiaries:

  • Avcon, which provides FAA-approved modifications and system integration.
  • Butler Avionics, which provides equipment manufacturing, sales, installation, and services.
  • Butler National or BNC-Tempe, which provides commercial, and defense electronics products to OEMs, contractors, and government agencies.
  • KC Machine, which is a certified machine shop and machine tool repair company.

Products include aerial surveillance products, displays, sensors, and transponders, among others. Modifications include the ability to install doors, TCAS systems, ISR systems, extended fuel tanks, and radar systems, while special mission electronics capabilities include cabling, electronic control systems, test equipment, and gun control units for Apache and Blackhawk helicopters, as well as land- and sea-based vehicles. If you are looking for a company with a huge exposure to commercial passenger jets, BNC is not for you. The company’s supplemental type certificate allowing it to carry out work includes small general aviation airplanes and business jets, including the Dassault Falcon jet. For Boeing, there are some STCs for older aircraft but nothing meaningful.

What makes the business really an odd one is the professional services. Those are in no way related to aerospace or defense. Through its subsidiary BNSC, the company operates the Boot Hill Casino & Resort in Dodge City, Kansas. It is not a straightforward combination with aerospace. The professional services segment generates revenues from casino gaming, sportsbook revenues, and non-gaming casino revenues.

So, it is a very odd combination that has no synergies whatsoever. The opportunities for BNC seem to lie in growing demand for aerospace solutions, but mostly for aircraft modifications and special mission electronics. I wouldn’t classify this as a company that will fare well in high demand for air travel. It is more special-purpose with exposure to defense. The casino provides some nice revenues, but I do not consider it to be a huge growth opportunity. If there are any changes in gambling laws, that could adversely affect the business, but I am not expecting any of that.

Aerospace Strength Shines While Gambling Results Are Pressured

Butler’s 10-Q filing shows that total revenues increased significantly by 9% to $23.3 million. Professional service revenues, which include the casino gaming revenues, casino non-gaming revenues, and sports wagering revenues, declined by 4.5%, while operating income declined 30% $1.68 million. The casino and sport wagering business is seeing some pressure across all its lines of business. This is primarily driven by the economic situation in agriculture, which normally brings in the gaming and wagering revenues. In response, the company has decided to sell one of its admin buildings for a gain on sale of $1.5 million.

Aerospace product revenues increased 20% to $14 million driven by $1 million in higher sales for the special mission electronics business and $1.2 million in aircraft modification revenues while aircraft avionics revenues remained more or less stable. Growth was driven by $4 million higher sales in North America and $0.5 million in Asia. This was partially offset by a $2.1 million decline in sales in Europe. Operating income rose from $2.5 million to $5.4 million indicating margin expansion from 21% to 38%

Total operating income increased from $4.9 million to $7 million indicating a margin expansion from 23% to 30%.

Aerospace As The Clear Growth Driver But With Risks

The clear growth driver is the aerospace segment. In some way, the casino business provides the relatively stable cash flow to grow the aerospace business. Potential growth drivers from new opportunities include a program for a bigger cargo door for the King Air turboprop, large aircraft system integrations, new radomes of business jets and special mission electronics including a next-generation minigun control unit. The company is also selling lower margin businesses, reorganizing the avionics business and bringing more production in-house. One example is the Newton facility in Kansas, which increases capacity 5x. So, the company is transforming into a high-margin niche aerospace system integrator. Key to the growth will be that the company can capitalize on it is supplemental type certificate pipeline programs such as the expanded cargo door and the radomes.

The company has substantially increased capacity. The risk of course is that it cannot fill the capacity and/or capitalize on the supplemental type certificate programs such as radome and extended cargo door programs. Those programs could be higher volume and are higher margins. So, failure to capitalize on those opportunities affects the growth and margin profile. Another pressure could be the economic realities for the casino business remaining depressed.

Butler National Corporation Has A Multi-Year Growth Trajectory

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and update the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

For Butler National Corporation, I have updated my growth assumptions. I am currently assuming 5.5% growth in revenues annually and that may actually be conservative growth rate. However, given the lack of analyst coverage and the niche nature of the business I rather be conservative. For EBITDA, I am assuming 10% annual growth as the aerospace business comes with higher EBITDA margins. Given the uncertain growth trajectory, sensitivity to several pipeline programs for growth and the exposure to the casino dynamics I am not applying an aerospace and defense EV/EBITDA. Instead, I use a valuation multiple that is equal to a third of peer group providing an additional layer of safety. That provides us with a $3.56 price target for 2027 indicating 19% upside with additional upside in the years ahead.

I believe that the company could grow EBITDA margins to around 35% and that is driven by the specialty nature of the aerospace business. If Butler executes in strong fashion, it will see strong margins and cash conversion. Even with share repurchases and debt reductions, the company will end up in a net cash position by the end of the decade. At the same time, there is the possibility that the maturities in 2028 will at least be partially refinanced. For that reason I am not assuming additional share repurchases beyond what is already modelled.

Conclusion: Butler National Corporation Stock Remains A Buy

I believe that Butler National Corporation stock remains a buy. The risk items include dependency on capitalization on STC (Supplemental Type Certificate) programs and soft results for the casino business. However, I do believe that the company has strong growth prospects in the aerospace business with an appreciable margin profile. As a result, I am reiterating my buy rating with a $3.56 price target.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JD.Com Is A Worthwhile Bet At This Valuation
data.description:
As you can see below, JD.com (JD) has seen a miserable past 52 weeks. The stock has fallen more than 25% and based on a quick glance at the chart, it doesn't seem it's in a hurry to recover those losses. Today, we'll take a look at their financials and recent developments to see if there is significant fundamental opportunity in JD.com at current levels. The company did report earnings quite a while ago but at the same time, they're not reporting again until early March and so I believe initiating coverage now is still appropriate.

Below, it is shown that JD.com's fundamentals are overall shaky with their margins getting weighed on by new businesses and cash flows being weak. While their push into logistics and other businesses may be a bit of a large gamble, it seems the company has adequate capital to buy time for these investments to payoff. Furthermore, with the valuation being at very depressed levels, I view the overall fundamental setup to be positive and so I'm initiating with a buy rating.

Revenue Analysis

Firstly, before we start with an analysis of their revenues, note that USD figures differ slightly platform to platform due to exchange rate discrepancies but the overall picture should be the same. For 2025 Q3, JD.com reported net revenues of RMB229.1 billion or $42 billion. This represents a growth rate of around 15% and you can see in the above chart that while this is a deceleration from Q2, this growth rate is still very respectable relative to levels we have seen in the past few years. Therefore, while consumer confidence in China is still far below 2021 levels, the company has shown that business activity has signs of improvement. Note that they were also able to beat consensus by about $860 million and so their top line was much better than expected.

While JD.com is of course product revenue dominated since it operates a large retail e-commerce platform, you can see that the strongest growth driver for the company is now services. Both on the multiyear timeframe and for Q3 YoY, net service revenue growth is outpacing net product revenue growth with the company increasing their presence in logistics. Is this increased focus on logistics the right direction for the company? The answer to that is a bit unclear in my view and we'll discuss this below.

Margin Under Pressure

Currently, JD retail is growing a bit sluggishly but their operating margin has actually expanded from 5.2% to 5.9% YoY. While product sales obviously come with relatively thin margins, this improvement is a signal that this segment is performing in a respectable manner. Here's why I'm not overly sure that the push into logistics is going to payoff in the near term.

While growth in JD Logistics greatly outpaced their JD Retail segment, you can see that operating income actually shrunk from already quite low levels. The logistics business is not a particularly easy one and is overall known to be quite capital intensive and so you can see that the profitability of this growth driver is really not there yet. As they continue to scale, it may be a different story down the road but at this point, bottom line results are getting weighed on.

As for their new businesses, revenue growth is sky high but overall contributions to the company remain low at this point. While, I'm definitely not against investments for future growth, the company does seem to be bleeding cash significantly in their new businesses. The RMB15.7 billion operating loss is a major widening YoY and almost completely offsets the operating income in Retail and Logistics and so at this point, JD.com seems to be making a pretty large gamble with their new businesses.

In my view, currently they are in a high-risk, high-reward phase since if they can make logistics and these new businesses profitable, they could be in good shape. At the end of they day, they reported a company-wide adjusted operating margin of just 0.1%, down from 5.0% YoY and their adjusted EPS saw a 56% decline to $0.53. Therefore, there are good reasons to be at least a little cautious on their strategy in my view.

Key Metrics Deteriorate

When looking at some other key metrics, there are signs of business deterioration for JD.com. For TTM inventory turnover days, there was been a considerable increase YoY to 35.8 as a sign that demand isn't really there or that there is poor inventory management. Products are essentially sitting in their warehouses longer.

For TTM accounts payable turnover days there was a slight increase YoY. With their cash balance being at RMB113 billion as of September 30th, it seems that they aren't having trouble paying bills and the increase in A/P turnover days likely just reflects better negotiated terms of payment. However, the good news ends there.

For TTM accounts receivable turnover days, there was a very meaningful increase from 5.8 to 8.3 YoY. This is a signal of weak and inefficient collections and of course that isn't a good sign for their cash flows by any means.

As you can see above, TTM FCFs have plunged by over 60% as a signal that the company's cash generation is currently weak. There was significant CAPEX in the TTM but note that TTM adjusted operating cash flows were also quite weak vs 2024 levels and so the FCF weakness isn't just because of their new business investments. Overall, I would say that these key metrics show more reasons for caution.

Recent Developments

Consumer Confidence Remains Low

While there are some signs that consumer confidence in China is starting to recover, it is a long way back to 2021 levels. Overall, for an e-commerce based company like JD.com, this shows that the near term outlook is murky right now and so while business activity may continue to bounce back, investors will likely need to be patient. With the geopolitical environment being rather unstable, I would expect a potentially bumpy recovery from here.

Some Capital Developments

In early January, there were two notable developments regarding the company's capital. It was reported that for CY2025, the company bought back around $3 billion in stock. As discussed earlier, JD.com's TTM FCF's plunged YoY and so their significant share repurchases does provide a signal that management remains confident on the company's prospects and they likely believe their stock is quite cheap currently.

Another important development is that the company is planning to raise $1.4 billion in a dim sum bond sale to lock in cheap funding. As stated earlier, JD.com's cash pile is significant as of September 30th, and so this potential offering likely isn't because of a liquidity crunch and is more of a matter of getting a good rate on capital for future use.

Valuation Is Depressed

In today's article, JD.com's fundamentals were shown to be a bit shaky. While logistics and new businesses help to power respectable top line growth, margins were under pressure and cash flows plunged. Moving forward, the slow recovery in China's consumer confidence is also not doing them any favors. However, for long term investors, I believe the valuation is simply a bit too cheap to ignore right now.

While the multiple has been lower in early to mid 2024, the 9.164 reading now still shows the stock is very cheaply valued. There has been significant multiple contraction since early 2025 and according to Seeking Alpha, JD.com's non-GAAP forward P/E ratio is now at a ~37% discount to the consumer discretionary sector as of this writing.

I would say that despite the weak profitability and undesirable macro environment right now, there is still a good chance that the company's bets on logistics and new businesses will payoff over the long run and I view the valuation to be missing out on that side of the picture. Therefore, I believe the risk/reward is net positive for investors at this time.

Takeaway

There is no question that JD.com is experiencing pain right now. What I believe the valuation fails to capture is the potential that this short term pain could lead to long term gains. While the profitability and cash flows are justified worries, the company also still has a good amount of capital and their planned bond offering shows they should have access to more funds if needed. Therefore, JD.com's new businesses have time to gain traction and that improves their odds of success. For patient investors, I view the overall risk/reward setup to be attractive based on the deep-value valuation and so I'm initiating the stock at a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Producer Price Index: Wholesale Inflation Up 0.5% In December
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest report on the Producer Price Index (PPI) shows that wholesale inflation for final demand increased by 0.5% in December. This uptick was higher than the expected 0.2% growth and follows a 0.2% increase in November. On an annual basis, the headline PPI was unchanged at 3.0% year-over-year, coming in above the forecast of 2.7%.

Core PPI, which excludes food and energy prices, was up 0.7% in December after a flat reading in November. This was higher than the expected 0.2% monthly growth. On an annual basis, core PPI heated up to 3.3% in December from 3.1% in November, coming in above the projected 2.9% reading.

Producer Price Index: Finished Goods

The BLS shifted its focus to the "final demand" PPI series in 2014, but data for these series extend only back to November 2009 for headline PPI and April 2010 for core PPI.

Since our analysis emphasizes longer-term trends, we continue to track the legacy PPI for finished goods, which the BLS still includes in its monthly updates. As a later overlay chart will illustrate, the final demand and finished goods indexes remain highly correlated.

In December, the PPI for finished goods was flat month-over-month, down from 1.0% in November. Year-over-year, headline PPI for finished goods cooled to 1.9% from 2.6% in November.

Meanwhile, core PPI for finished goods was up 0.3% on the month, up from 0.2% in November. On an annual basis, core PPI for finished goods heated up to 3.4% from 3.2% in November.

Producer Price Index (PPI) vs. Consumer Price Index (CPI)

Both PPI and CPI illustrate monthly price changes. However, as their names suggest, the Producer Price Index measures price changes from the producer perspective, whereas the Consumer Price Index measures price changes from the consumer perspective.

PPI is thought to be a leading indicator of consumer inflation because, for the most part, when producers pay more for goods and services they are likely to pass along those higher costs to the consumer.

With that being said, during the 2020 recession, producers were unable to pass along price increases, demonstrating the higher volatility of core PPI than core CPI. This relationship is further illustrated in the next chart.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
